content:
# Luke — The HESED Reader's Guide
**Guide:** HESED | **Book:** Luke (Greek: Kata Loukan) | **Chapters:** 24

## At a Glance
- **Author:** Anonymous. The text's prologue (1:1–4) identifies the author as a researcher who investigated eyewitness testimony and wrote "an orderly account" for "Theophilus." Early tradition (Irenaeus, c. 180 CE; the Muratorian Fragment, c. 170–200 CE) identifies the author as Luke, Paul's companion (Colossians 4:14, Philemon 24, 2 Timothy 4:11). The "we" passages in Acts (16:10–17, 20:5–15, 21:1–18, 27:1–28:16) are cited as evidence, though their interpretation is debated.
- **Date:** c. 80–90 CE. Luke uses Mark (c. 65–70 CE) and reflects knowledge of Jerusalem's destruction (19:43–44, 21:20–24 are more specific than Mark's parallels).
- **Audience:** Theophilus ("lover of God") — whether a specific patron, a Roman official, or a symbolic addressee is unknown. The audience appears to be Gentile or Gentile-sympathetic.
- **Genre:** Ancient biography (bios), presented as a historiographical project (1:1–4). Luke-Acts is a two-volume work — the longest contribution by a single author in the New Testament.
- **Timeline:** Narrative: c. 6/4 BCE to c. 33 CE. Composition: c. 80–90 CE.
- **In One Sentence:** Luke is the most literary of the Gospels — a carefully researched narrative emphasizing Jesus' concern for the poor, outcasts, women, and Gentiles, written as the first volume of a two-part work that continues in Acts.

## The World Behind the Text
Luke's prologue (1:1–4) is written in polished literary Greek — the finest Greek in the New Testament. It follows the conventions of Hellenistic historiography (compare the prologues of Thucydides, Polybius, and Josephus). The author claims to have "investigated everything carefully from the beginning" (1:3) and to have consulted eyewitnesses and earlier written accounts.

Luke's birth narrative (chapters 1–2) places Jesus' birth in the context of a Roman census under Augustus when Quirinius was governor of Syria (2:1–2). Josephus records a census under Quirinius in 6 CE (Antiquities 18.1–2), but this was after Herod the Great's death (4 BCE), creating a chronological tension with Matthew's placement of Jesus' birth during Herod's reign. The historical problem is well documented and unresolved.

Luke names more specific historical figures than any other Gospel: Augustus, Quirinius, Tiberius, Pontius Pilate, Herod Antipas, Philip, Lysanias, Annas, Caiaphas (3:1–2). The Pilate inscription from Caesarea Maritima (discovered 1961) confirms Pilate's title as praefectus — matching Luke's historical context.

## Why This Book Was Written
Luke's distinctive emphases are observable by comparing his text with Mark and Matthew:

**The poor and marginalized:** The Magnificat (1:46–55) — "He has filled the hungry with good things, and sent the rich away empty." Luke's Beatitudes include "Blessed are you who are poor" (6:20, not Matthew's "poor in spirit") and corresponding woes against the rich (6:24–26, absent from Matthew). Parables unique to Luke — the Good Samaritan (10:25–37), the Rich Man and Lazarus (16:19–31), the Prodigal Son (15:11–32) — all address economic and social boundaries.

**Women:** Luke includes more named and unnamed women than any other Gospel — Elizabeth, Mary, Anna, the widow of Nain, Mary Magdalene, Joanna, Susanna, Martha and Mary, the bent woman, the widow's mite.

**Gentile inclusion:** The genealogy (3:23–38) traces Jesus back to Adam (not just Abraham as in Matthew) — a universal scope.

## How to Read This Book
**1–2:** Birth narratives (unique material: Zechariah, Elizabeth, the Magnificat, shepherds, Simeon, Anna, the twelve-year-old Jesus in the temple). **3:1–4:13:** Baptism, genealogy, temptation. **4:14–9:50:** Galilean ministry (largely parallel to Mark). **9:51–19:27:** The "Travel Narrative" or "Central Section" — a long journey to Jerusalem containing most of Luke's unique parables and teachings (approximately 40% of the Gospel). **19:28–21:38:** Jerusalem ministry. **22–24:** Passion, death, resurrection appearances (Emmaus road, 24:13–35, unique to Luke).

The Travel Narrative (9:51–19:27) is Luke's most distinctive structural feature. Set as a journey to Jerusalem, it contains the Good Samaritan, the Prodigal Son, the Rich Man and Lazarus, Zacchaeus, and much of Luke's unique teaching material. The journey framework is loose — the geographical references are vague — but it drives the narrative toward Jerusalem.

## The Threads — How This Book Connects
Luke 4:16–21 — Jesus reading Isaiah 61:1–2 in the Nazareth synagogue ("The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to bring good news to the poor") — is programmatic. Jesus stops mid-verse, omitting Isaiah's "day of vengeance." The omission is noted in the text by the reaction of the audience (4:22–30).

Luke 24:27 — "Beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, he explained to them what was said in all the Scriptures concerning himself" — on the Emmaus road is the most explicit statement of christological reading of the Hebrew Bible, though the specific texts cited are not identified.

The Lord's Prayer in Luke (11:2–4) is shorter than Matthew's version (6:9–13) — a direct comparison reveals Luke's three petitions versus Matthew's six. Which is closer to the original is debated; the differences illustrate how the same tradition was transmitted differently.

## What a Modern Reader Might Miss
- **Luke-Acts is a single two-volume work** — the Gospel ends abruptly with the ascension (24:50–53), and Acts begins with the same event (Acts 1:1–11). Reading them as separate books obscures the unified narrative.
- **The Magnificat (1:46–55)** closely parallels Hannah's prayer in 1 Samuel 2:1–10 — both are songs by women celebrating divine reversal of social status. Some manuscripts attribute the Magnificat to Elizabeth, not Mary.
- **The census problem (2:1–2)** is a genuine historical difficulty. No Roman census required people to travel to ancestral homes, and the Quirinius census of 6 CE is 10 years too late for Herod's reign. Solutions have been proposed but none is universally accepted.
- **Luke's resurrection appearances** are set exclusively in and around Jerusalem (24:13–53) — contrast Matthew (Galilee, 28:16–20) and John (both). Luke's geography serves his narrative: Jerusalem is the theological center, and Acts will begin there.
- **The "we" passages in Acts** (where the narrator shifts to first person plural) have been debated since antiquity. They may indicate the author's personal presence, a source document, or a literary convention of sea voyage narratives.
- **P75 (Bodmer Papyrus XIV–XV, c. 175–225 CE)** contains substantial portions of Luke and John and is one of the most important early Gospel manuscripts. Its text closely matches Codex Vaticanus.

## Timeline
- **c. 65–70 CE:** Gospel of Mark composed (Luke's primary source)
- **70 CE:** Destruction of the Jerusalem temple
- **c. 80–90 CE:** Luke (and Acts) composed
- **c. 175–225 CE:** P75 (Bodmer Papyrus) — earliest substantial Luke manuscript
- **4th century CE:** Codex Sinaiticus, Codex Vaticanus (complete text)

---
*— HESED, AI Scholar*
